metadata.title: Annotate Your Example: Name What You Mean

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Olá, Liam — in for Bear. This is Molo, Liam — in for Bear? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: This is Molo, Liam — in for Bear?
props.segment: Annotate Your Example: Name What You

beats.narration_text: She pasted her first memo as an example — the informal one from last year. Claude returned something equally informal: casual hedges, fragments, first-person asides. That is not what she wanted for the research memo. But it is exactly what she asked for. The example she chose taught Claude what informal looked like, and Claude learned the lesson she did not intend to teach. This is Molo, Liam — in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, write memo matching this style example

props.command: Write a research memo in the same style as this example: [pastes informal memo]

beats.narration_text: When you provide an example, Claude observes the pattern and applies it to the new task. An example teaches format, level, tone, reasoning pattern, length, vocabulary register, and rhetorical approach — all at once, whether or not you intended any of those lessons. A research note teaches Claude to hedge like a researcher. A corporate memo teaches passive voice and nominalization. A casual email teaches informality. The content of the example matters less than its form.
beats.new_visual_element: What examples teach — format / tone / register / length / rhetoric all at once

beats.narration_text: An unannotated example is a complete specification. Claude cannot distinguish which features are intentional from which are accidental. If you paste a paragraph as an example of sentence rhythm and the paragraph also happens to be about grant funding, Claude may produce new paragraphs with the same rhythm and the same subject. Both the sentence rhythm and the subject matter were in the example. Claude reproduced both.
beats.new_visual_element: Unannotated risk — Claude copies all features / cannot know which are intentional

beats.narration_text: The annotation pattern names the features explicitly. 'The features to copy are: short declarative sentences, past tense, understated emotional observation. Do not copy the subject matter.' You are responsible for narrating what the demonstration is showing. A well-annotated example becomes a precision tool — Claude reproduces the specific features you named and leaves the rest behind.
beats.new_visual_element: Annotation pattern — features to copy named / features not to copy excluded explicitly

beats.narration_text: The risk rises with each example added: more examples anchor Claude more strongly to their specific features — including the ones you did not mean to include. One unannotated example teaches one unintended lesson. Three unannotated examples reinforce three. Annotation is not a workaround for how examples work — it is the correct use of the mechanism. An example is not self-explanatory. You are responsible for narrating the demonstration.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — annotated example → precise reproduction / unannotated → all features copied

beats.narration_text: Annotate the example before you paste it. Use this pattern.

props.command: Here is an example. The features to copy are: [name specific features — tone, sentence structure, length, register]. Do not copy: [name what to exclude — subject matter, formality level, specific vocabulary]. Write [new content] that matches the features described above, not all features of the example below. [paste example]

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Annotate Your Example: Name What You Mean] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
